threats.title: Arbitrary ERC20 Theft via AssignmentHook coinbase Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can steal ERC20 tokens from any legitimate proposer who has approved the AssignmentHook contract by exploiting the lack of binding between the prover's signature and the block's coinbase address.
threats.investigation_rationale: The AssignmentHook uses safeTransferFrom to pull the prover fee from meta.coinbase. The proposer controls params.coinbase. If a prover signs a generic assignment (metaHash = 0), the signature does not commit to the coinbase. An attacker can use this signed assignment and set params.coinbase to a victim's address, forcing the victim to pay the attacker's prover fee.
preconditions: Victim has granted ERC20 allowance to AssignmentHook
Prover signs an assignment with metaHash = 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Proposer can arbitrarily set the address from which ERC20 fees are pulled without victim signature
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a generic assignment is available in the mempool or off-chain

operational_questions: Do provers commonly sign assignments with metaHash = 0?
Do legitimate proposers maintain standing allowances to AssignmentHook?
validation_requirements: Construct a proposeBlock transaction using a victim's address as coinbase
Verify that safeTransferFrom successfully pulls funds from the victim

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a victim address with a standing ERC20 allowance to AssignmentHook
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Obtain a valid prover assignment with metaHash = 0 (e.g., from a public prover market)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call proposeBlock, setting params.coinbase to the victim's address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: AssignmentHook pulls the fee from the victim, subsidizing the attacker's block proposal
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None, can be executed unilaterally on-chain

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of funds for legitimate proposers, destroying trust in the proving market
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Stolen funds cannot be recovered; requires contract upgrade to fix
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Block proposing and prover fee payments

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected ERC20 transfers from proposer addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Attackers repeatedly using the same victim address for coinbase

threats.title: SGX Key Expiry Bypass via Self-Replacement
threats.hypothesis_description: An SGX enclave can bypass the mandatory 180-day key rotation requirement by signing a proof that replaces its current instance address with the exact same address, resetting the expiry timer indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _replaceInstance function updates the validSince timestamp to block.timestamp but fails to check if newInstance is different from oldInstance. This defeats the side-channel protection mechanism that requires periodic key rotation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, uint64(block.timestamp)); without checking newInstance != oldInstance

preconditions: Attacker controls a valid registered SGX enclave instance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Key rotation mechanism allows rotating to the exact same key
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time before the instance expires

operational_questions: Does the SGX enclave code prevent signing a replacement to its own address?
Are there off-chain monitoring tools that would detect this?
validation_requirements: Submit a proof where newInstance == oldInstance
Verify that the validSince timestamp is updated and the instance remains valid

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Before the 180-day expiry, generate a proof with newInstance set to the current instance address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The contract resets the validSince timer, granting another 180 days of validity without key rotation
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires modifying the SGX enclave client to sign its own address

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Degradation of SGX security guarantees against side-channel attacks
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to enforce newInstance != oldInstance

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Instances remaining active longer than 180 days

threats.title: Assigned Prover Griefing and Bond Slashing via Lockout
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can maliciously contest a correct proof submitted by the assigned prover. The assigned prover is explicitly locked out from submitting the higher-tier proof required to resolve the contest, causing them to lose half their liveness bond when a third party resolves it.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _checkProverPermission function reverts if the assigned prover attempts to submit any proof other than the initial one (tid == 1 && ts.tier == 0). If their correct proof is contested, they cannot defend it. When a third party submits the higher-tier proof, ts.prover changes, and the assigned prover is penalized during verification.
preconditions: Assigned prover submits a correct initial proof
Attacker has enough TKO to post a contest bond

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assigned prover is explicitly forbidden from participating in contests on their own block
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While a block is in the proving phase

operational_questions: Is the penalty for ts.prover != assignedProver intended even when the assigned prover was originally correct?
Will provers stop participating if they can be systematically griefed?
validation_requirements: Simulate assigned prover submitting correct proof
Simulate attacker contesting it
Verify assigned prover cannot submit higher tier proof
Verify assigned prover loses half liveness bond upon verification

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for blocks proven by their assigned prover
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit a contest transaction with a dummy transition hash
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for a third-party prover to submit the higher-tier proof resolving the contest
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The assigned prover loses half their liveness bond, creating a disincentive to be an assigned prover

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Destruction of the proving market economics; assigned provers will refuse to operate
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to fix permission logic

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High rate of contested blocks where the final state root matches the initial state root
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Assigned provers losing liveness bonds despite submitting correct transitions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific addresses systematically contesting valid proofs

threats.title: Predictable Randomness Allows Bypassing High-Security Tiers
threats.hypothesis_description: The minimum proof tier for a block is determined using predictable L1 chain attributes (block.prevrandao, block.number). A proposer can compute the required tier off-chain and selectively withhold proposals that require expensive, high-security tiers (like SGX_ZKVM).
threats.investigation_rationale: By bypassing high-security tiers, an attacker can ensure their blocks are only protected by lower-tier proofs, reducing the cost and complexity of submitting fraudulent state transitions if they compromise the lower-tier systems.
preconditions: Proposer is willing to delay their block proposal by one or more L1 blocks

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Randomness used for critical security parameter is entirely predictable before transaction inclusion

operational_questions: What is the statistical distribution of tiers in production vs expected?
Are proposers actively optimizing for lower tiers?
validation_requirements: Write a script that predicts the minTier for the next block
Verify that withholding the transaction until a favorable block changes the minTier

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Calculate the expected minTier for the current L1 block off-chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If the tier is SGX_ZKVM, do not broadcast the proposeBlock transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for the next L1 block and recalculate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Broadcast the transaction only when the required tier is the cheapest/lowest security option

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol never undergoes the intended high-security random audits, weakening overall rollup security
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to integrate a secure VRF

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Statistical absence of high-tier blocks compared to the configured probability
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposers consistently landing blocks on low-tier L1 blocks
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Transaction timing correlated with prevrandao values

threats.title: Guardian Proof Manipulation via Incomplete Approval Hash
threats.hypothesis_description: The hash signed by guardians for multisig approval does not include the proof data. The last guardian to reach the threshold can unilaterally inject arbitrary proof data (such as the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic value) when triggering the actual proveBlock call, bypassing multisig consensus on the proof payload.
threats.investigation_rationale: GuardianProver.approve hashes only _meta and _tran. The _proof parameter is passed directly to TaikoL1.proveBlock by the guardian who crosses the minGuardians threshold. This allows that specific guardian to return the liveness bond to the assigned prover without the agreement of the other guardians.
preconditions: Attacker waits to be the threshold-triggering approval

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Multisig approval hash does not cover the entire payload sent to the target contract

operational_questions: Do guardians currently submit proof data, or is it always empty?
Can a single compromised guardian collude with an assigned prover to extract bonds?
validation_requirements: Simulate N-1 guardians approving with empty proof data
Simulate the Nth guardian approving with RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND data
Verify the liveness bond is returned despite N-1 guardians not authorizing it

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker (controlling one guardian) colludes with an assigned prover
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for other guardians to submit approvals for a valid transition
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit the final approval transaction, injecting RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND into _proof.data
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: TaikoL1 processes the proof and returns the liveness bond to the colluding assigned prover
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control of one guardian key and timing the final transaction

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Partial compromise of guardian multisig integrity
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to include _proof in the approval hash

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: At the moment of threshold approval

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Specific guardian consistently submitting the final threshold transaction
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Collusion between a specific guardian and assigned provers

threats.title: SGX Instance Duplication via Missing Registration State Update
threats.hypothesis_description: When an SGX instance rotates its key, the new instance address is not marked as registered. This allows the enclave to re-register the same new address via the attestation flow, obtaining multiple valid instance IDs for a single key.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _replaceInstance function fails to set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. An attacker can use the Automata DCAP attestation for the new key to call registerInstance(), which will succeed and create a duplicate instance ID. This breaks the 1-to-1 mapping invariant and allows the enclave to juggle multiple IDs to bypass operational limits.
preconditions: Attacker controls a valid SGX enclave
Attacker can generate a valid DCAP attestation for the new key

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State variable tracking uniqueness is not updated during the lifecycle of the object

operational_questions: Can an enclave generate an attestation quote for a key it just rotated to?
Does the protocol rely on instance ID uniqueness for any off-chain monitoring or slashing?
validation_requirements: Rotate an SGX key via verifyProof
Call registerInstance with an attestation for the new key
Verify a second instance ID is created for the same address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit a proof that rotates the SGX key from oldInstance to newInstance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Generate a DCAP attestation quote for newInstance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: SgxVerifier creates a new instance ID for newInstance, giving the enclave two active IDs
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires custom SGX client to extract quotes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Corruption of the SGX registry, potential bypass of instance-level rate limits or slashing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix state tracking

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple instance IDs mapping to the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid registration of instances immediately following a rotation

threats.title: Proposer Griefing via Reverting Prover Fallback
threats.hypothesis_description: A malicious prover can cause a proposer's L1 transaction to revert by using a smart contract that rejects ETH transfers. This wastes the proposer's gas and prevents them from proposing blocks.
threats.investigation_rationale: AssignmentHook uses sendEther to pay the prover fee. If the transfer fails (e.g., the prover contract reverts), sendEther reverts the entire transaction. A malicious prover can advertise assignments, wait for proposers to use them, and then revert the payment, causing a griefing DoS.
preconditions: Prover fee is paid in ETH
Assigned prover is a malicious smart contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External call failure propagates to the main protocol workflow

operational_questions: Are provers allowed to be smart contracts?
Is there a penalty for provers who cause proposers' transactions to fail?
validation_requirements: Deploy a prover contract with a reverting receive function
Sign an assignment and have a proposer submit it
Verify the proposer's transaction reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy a malicious prover contract that reverts on receiving ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Sign highly attractive prover assignments (e.g., low fees) to bait proposers
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Proposers include the assignment in proposeBlock
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The transaction reverts during the fee payment, wasting proposer gas

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Proposers waste gas, potentially halting block production if widespread
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires proposers to manually blacklist malicious provers off-chain

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High rate of reverted proposeBlock transactions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Specific provers associated with failed proposals
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Bait-and-switch pricing in off-chain prover markets

threats.title: Guardian Emergency Recovery Blocked by Proving Pause
threats.hypothesis_description: If the protocol is paused during an operational crisis, the Guardians (the ultimate fallback mechanism) are also blocked from submitting corrective proofs, preventing emergency state recovery.
threats.investigation_rationale: The proveBlock function is gated by whenProvingNotPaused. Guardian proofs must go through proveBlock. Therefore, pausing the system to stop an attack simultaneously disables the mechanism designed to fix the state, forcing administrators to unpause the system (re-exposing it) to allow recovery.
preconditions: Protocol proving is paused via pauseProving(true)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Emergency recovery mechanism is disabled by the emergency pause mechanism

operational_questions: Is it intended that guardians cannot operate while paused?
How does the team plan to recover state if a malicious proof is pending and the system is paused?
validation_requirements: Pause proving
Attempt to submit a guardian proof via GuardianProver.approve
Verify the transaction reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Admin detects it and pauses proving
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Guardians attempt to submit the correct proof to override the malicious one
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Guardian transaction reverts, leaving the system deadlocked
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None (this is an operational deadlock, not a direct exploit)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Prolonged protocol downtime during incident response

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted guardian proofs during a pause
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: System remaining paused longer than necessary due to recovery inability

threats.title: Timelock Bypass by Compromised Admin Role
threats.hypothesis_description: The TaikoTimelockController allows any address with the TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE to bypass the minimum delay entirely. If the Security Council multisig (which holds this role) is compromised, attackers can execute malicious upgrades instantly.
threats.investigation_rationale: The getMinDelay override explicitly returns 0 for the admin role. While intended for emergency response, it creates a single point of failure where an off-chain compromise translates to instant, unmitigable on-chain protocol takeover, defeating the purpose of the timelock.
deployment_assumptions: Security Council multisig is granted TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE
preconditions: Security Council multisig is compromised or acts maliciously

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Timelock delay is conditionally disabled for specific roles

operational_questions: Is the Security Council multisig threshold sufficient to prevent compromise?
Are there off-chain monitoring systems that can detect and respond to instant execution?
validation_requirements: Verify the deployment script grants the role
Simulate an instant execution by the admin role

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise the Security Council multisig off-chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute the payload instantly in the same block, bypassing the 7-day delay
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain protocol assets or alter core logic

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of funds and protocol integrity

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Social engineering or advanced key theft

threats.title: Proving Window Indefinite Extension via Pause Toggling
threats.hypothesis_description: A compromised or malicious chain_pauser can indefinitely extend the exclusive proving window for an assigned prover by repeatedly toggling the pause state, stalling block verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The proving window is calculated using max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt). Every time the protocol is unpaused, lastUnpausedAt is updated to the current timestamp. By pausing and unpausing periodically, the window never expires, preventing other provers from stepping in.
preconditions: chain_pauser role is compromised or acts maliciously
Assigned prover colludes by not submitting a proof

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Global state variable reset allows indefinite extension of a local timeout

operational_questions: Why does unpausing reset the window for ALL blocks instead of just adding the paused duration?
Is the chain_pauser a single key or a multisig?
validation_requirements: Propose a block
Wait for the window to almost expire
Pause and unpause
Verify the window is extended

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Collude with an assigned prover to withhold proofs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false) before the proving window expires
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat indefinitely, preventing any other prover from verifying the block and stalling the chain

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: L2 chain halts as blocks cannot be verified
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires owner to revoke chain_pauser role

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Blocks stuck in unproven state despite active third-party provers
